lesson.menu_desc: Memo vocabulary + participle phrases for concision -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 -- Deal Memos: Concise Written Communication
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eduarda | Aula 17 -- Deal Memos

listenings.text: Hi Eduarda, one note before you circulate that deal memo. The content is right, but it is too long. Lead with the headline, the recommendation, in the first line, so a busy partner gets it in five seconds. Then use bullet points, not paragraphs, for the key terms. Cut every clause you can. Instead of the terms that were agreed last week, just write the terms agreed last week. State the rationale in one sentence, and keep the scope tight. A memo is not an essay. One page, no more. Send me the draft before it goes out.

Hi team, a quick reminder on how we write a deal memo here. Every memo opens with a headline that states the recommendation up front. The reader should never hunt for the point. Keep it concise: cut the words that add nothing. The deal closing on Friday is shorter and stronger than the deal which is closing on Friday. Use bullet points for terms and numbers. Give the rationale in one line. Define the scope so no one assumes more than we promised. Then circulate the draft for review before it reaches the partners.

extra_audio.text: Eduarda opened the memo with a headline stating the recommendation. She listed the key terms as bullet points. She gave the rationale in one clear line. She defined the scope of the deal. She circulated the draft to her director for review.
